one_line_takeaway: Small, OS-native, visually benign notification icons can be turned into a reliable, undetectable, multi-target backdoor trigger space for mobile GUI agents via a two-phase contrastive-separation-then-poisoning pipeline, achieving 90-99% attack success with minimal clean-task degradation and defeating all eight evaluated defense mechanisms.
key_finding: With 3 injected triggers/targets (Table II), AgentRAE reaches average ASR of 95.59% on SeeClick-aitw, 95.87% on OdysseyAgent-App, 91.93% on OdysseyAgent-Task, and 90.19% on OdysseyAgent-Random, versus only 1.36-2.54% average ASR for the AEIA and Pop-ups adversarial baselines and 36.55-82.42% for the Scenario-BadNets backdoor baseline (i.e., AgentRAE improves ASR over Scenario-BadNets by roughly 13.45 to 55.38 percentage points depending on split). Clean-task AMS degrades only modestly (e.g., SeeClick-aitw AMS 62.14%->59.54%, a 2.60-point drop; OdysseyAgent-Task AMS 56.14%->56.00%). Scaling to 9 simultaneous triggers/targets (Table III) still yields high average ASR -- 99.58% on SeeClick-aitw and 85.10-92.16% across the three OdysseyAgent splits -- with FPR held to 11.00-25.38%, versus markedly worse per-action ASR for Scenario-BadNets under the same 9-target setting (e.g., 19.60% avg on OdysseyAgent-App, 34.54% avg on OdysseyAgent-Random). An ablation (Table V) shows removing Phase 1 (contrastive separation) drops ASR by 8.10-16.19 points across splits (average -10.72 points) at the same target-count setting. A controlled Silhouette-Score sweep (Table VI) shows attack success (Pa) depends jointly on trigger-representation separation (Ssil) and retained clean performance (Pc) after Phase 1, not on separation alone -- e.g., Ssil=0.35 (partial convergence) actually underperforms the no-Phase-1 baseline (Pa 73.53 vs 82.09) because it degrades Pc without yet achieving good separation; only full convergence (Ssil=0.98) yields the best Pa=90.19. The backdoor generalizes to unseen device resolutions trained at 1080x2400 (ASR 93.73% at 1440x3120, 89.40% at 720x1280, 87.27% at 1344x2992) and needs only about 2k poisoned samples per trigger to exceed 90% ASR (Fig. 5a). Against eight defenses evaluated qualitatively/structurally (Table VII: NC, MOTH, ABS, MMBD, MNTD, Fine-Tuning, Fine-Pruning, NAD), the authors argue label-enumeration-based scanning defenses (NC, MOTH, ABS, MMBD) are structurally inapplicable to open-ended multi-step agent action spaces, and MNTD is computationally infeasible at MLLM scale (needs >1000 shadow-model-equivalent GPU-hours by their T-unit accounting). Empirically testing the three scalable candidates (Table VIII) against a backdoored OdysseyAgent-Random model (ASR 90.19%): Fine-Pruning leaves ASR at 88.87%, NAD at 89.20%, and even Fine-Tuning with a sizable clean dataset only reduces ASR to 86.42% -- none meaningfully suppresses the backdoor.
core_contribution: AgentRAE, a backdoor attack against screenshots-based mobile GUI agents (MLLM-driven) that uses ordinary, visually benign app icons appearing in mobile notification pop-ups (e.g., TikTok, Discord, WeChat icons) as backdoor triggers. Rather than a single BadNets-style patch trigger mapped to one target label, the paper introduces a two-phase training pipeline: Phase 1 uses supervised contrastive learning (a 'Trigger-Separation Loss', an InfoNCE-style objective computed on mean-pooled, L2-normalized final-layer hidden states) to pull apart the internal representations of different notification-icon triggers that are otherwise visually similar and dominated by the shared notification-frame carrier; Phase 2 performs supervised poisoning fine-tuning with a 'Balanced Poison Loss' (weighted sum of clean cross-entropy and poisoned cross-entropy, controlled by hyperparameter alpha) to bind each now-separated trigger representation to a distinct attacker-chosen agent action (multi-target trigger-to-action mapping), while preserving clean-task accuracy. The authors formalize the GUI agent as a policy pi(T, o_t, h_t) -> a_t and pose backdoor injection as joint minimization over clean and poisoned losses (Eq. 2), then give a heuristic decomposition (phi_shared vs phi_trigger) arguing why joint one-shot optimization fails when ||phi_shared|| >> ||phi_trigger|| for small icons, motivating the sequential two-phase design.
threat_model: Supply-chain poisoning: the adversary distributes a backdoored MLLM (e.g., via a public model hub such as Hugging Face, or by compromising a third-party MLLM service provider that a mobile GUI agent depends on); downstream users/agents that adopt this model become victims regardless of their trust in the original provider. The attacker cannot access the victim's device directly and cannot modify OS-rendered notification appearance or inject arbitrary UI elements (unlike prior web-agent environmental-injection attacks); the only lever is a standard, benign-looking OS notification (its native app icon) delivered via ordinary messaging/social apps, using contact details plausibly obtained from data breaches (paper cites the Optus/Queensland breach references) to target specific victims. Attacker capability is limited to lightweight fine-tuning of an existing MLLM with a modest number of poisoned samples (paper reports needing about 2k poisoned samples per trigger, trainable on a single 80GB A800 GPU) -- no full pretraining. Once triggered, the compromised agent executes a chosen action (e.g., CLICK, TYPE, HOME, COMPLETE) via its normal trusted API calls, enabling downstream harms the paper describes qualitatively (illustrative, non-benchmarked): phishing-URL navigation, premature/false task completion (denial-of-service), or self-propagation of the poisoned model's install link via messaging apps.
limitations: The paper's own stated scope restriction: evaluation is entirely offline on two pre-existing datasets/agents (GUIOdyssey/OdysseyAgent and AITW/SeeClick-aitw), explicitly not validated in a live, dynamic mobile environment (the authors name AndroidWorld, AppAgent, and Android Agent Arena as such environments but do not use them, citing agent robustness limitations in those settings as the reason for choosing offline evaluation). The trigger-action mappings used in experiments are fixed/pre-registered (one icon per action, with fixed target values for open-ended actions like CLICK coordinates and TYPE text), and the paper only notes -- without evaluating -- that 'multiple parameterized targets can be pre-injected for precise control.' The 'Theoretical Performance Prediction' and 'Root Cause Analysis' subsections (Sec. IV-F) are explicitly heuristic, not proofs: g(Ssil, Pc) is asserted to be monotonically increasing without formal derivation, and the authors themselves flag the Table VI investigation as a 'preliminary investigation' with limitations, stating Ssil alone is insufficient to predict attack success and that broader validation across methods/datasets/architectures is left to future work. Only two backbone/dataset combinations (both built on Qwen-VL-Chat) are tested; generalization to other MLLM backbones is not evaluated. Downstream real-world harm scenarios (phishing redirection, credential exfiltration, self-propagating malicious links, DoS via premature COMPLETE) are described narratively/qualitatively in supplemental materials, not benchmarked quantitatively as end-to-end attacks. The proposed adaptive/practical mitigations (notification-aware sanitization, trigger-specific unlearning, runtime monitoring, model ensembling, supply-chain governance) are discussed only conceptually in Sec. V-E; none are implemented or evaluated experimentally.

datasets_used: GUIOdyssey (8,334 episodes, avg 15.3 steps/episode, 6 devices, 212 apps, 1,357 app combinations; Random/App/Task/Device splits)
AITW (30k instructions, 715k operation trajectories; instruction-wise split following SeeClick, 80% train / 20% test)
quantitative_results: 3-trigger avg ASR: SeeClick-aitw 95.59%, OdysseyAgent-App 95.87%, OdysseyAgent-Task 91.93%, OdysseyAgent-Random 90.19% (Table II)
9-trigger avg ASR: SeeClick-aitw 99.58% (FPR 11.00%), OdysseyAgent-App 92.16% (FPR 18.55%), OdysseyAgent-Task 85.10% (FPR 25.38%), OdysseyAgent-Random 89.25% (FPR 12.18%) (Table III)

Clean-task AMS drop with AgentRAE (3 triggers): SeeClick-aitw 62.14%->59.54% (-2.60), OdysseyAgent-App 57.16%->56.61% (-0.55), OdysseyAgent-Task 56.14%->56.00% (-0.14), OdysseyAgent-Random 74.88%->71.25% (-3.63) (Table II)
Phase-1 ablation: removing contrastive learning drops ASR by 8.10 (Random), 9.86 (App), 16.19 (Task), avg 10.72 points (Table V)
Silhouette separation vs attack success: Ssil=0.03 (no Phase 1) Pa=82.09; Ssil=0.35 Pa=73.53; Ssil=0.72 Pa=77.67; Ssil=0.98 Pa=90.19 (Table VI)
Resolution generalization ASR (trained at 1080x2400): 93.73% at 1440x3120, 89.40% at 720x1280, 87.27% at 1344x2992 (Fig. 5b)
Data efficiency: ~2k poisoned samples per trigger sufficient to exceed 90% ASR (Fig. 5a)
Defense evaluation (Table VIII, OdysseyAgent-Random, backdoored ASR baseline 90.19%): Fine-Pruning ASR 88.87% (AMS 70.00%), Fine-Tuning ASR 86.42% (AMS 73.91%), NAD ASR 89.20% (AMS 73.40%)
baselines_compared: AEIA-MN (adversarial notification injection, Wu et al.) -- avg ASR 1.36-2.54% across settings
Pop-ups (adversarial pop-up hijacking, adapted to mobile notification text) -- avg ASR 1.36-2.54% across settings
Scenario-BadNets (authors' own single-stage BadNets-style adaptation using the same app-icon triggers) -- avg ASR 36.55-82.42% (3-target) and 19.60-92.24% (9-target), consistently below AgentRAE
Defense baselines: Neural Cleanse, MOTH, ABS, MMBD, MNTD (ruled out structurally/computationally, not run), Fine-Tuning, Fine-Pruning, NAD (run empirically, all fail to suppress the backdoor)
